g.text: Talk outline:
0. Title page: "Master schema dissection: operation Data Engineer"
1. About page: who am I, what do I do
2. The story hook: your task as a Big Data Engineer it to create one big-table (OBTs)
3. Pain: a table with 100+ columns in denormalized form
4. Why? It's for performance reasons
3.1 DENORMALIZED? 🙀 100+ columns?? 😱
4.1 The unconfortable truth: to feed dashboards & stakeholders don't have to JOIN
5. DON'T PANIC!
2.1 Ask the audience, how many of them faced similar situations
13.3 Often only Facts sit in between Dimensions (star), yet there could be Dimensions connecting more facts (conformed dimension)
7. Tables are leaky abstractions
8. OLTP vs. OLAP
6. OBT often contains many entities in it
6.1 with many entities, there's more redundancy (horizontal redundancy is ok)
8.1 OLTP capture transactions: write-optimized, produces 'small' normalized tables, (almost) 1-1 with objects
5.1 Let's take a step back: break-down the problem
8.2 OLAP empowers analytics: read-optimized, aggregates data from multiple entities
7.1 A design element meant to simplify complexity by hiding underlying im plementation details but fails to do so completely
10. OBT is the artifact, you must work on THE DATA JOURNEY. Typically:
10.1 You have ERDs || Time Series as input
10.3 Create a Data Mart as an organised layer of Fact & Dimension Tables w/t (Star/Snowflake/Data Vault/Anchor) modeling
14. Understand the cardinality among these tables
- one-to-one ( +---+ )
- one-to-many ( +---< )
- many-to-many ( >---< )
13. Let's focus on a Star/snowflake modeling just because it tends to be the most common
9. Let's face the beast, columns in tables are not the same:
PKs: indentify the row but can take many forms: surrogate, composite, candidate, business
Dimensions: describe the attributes of an entity, but are subject to evolution over time
Measures: represent point-in-time metrics of an entity or relationship
Time Granularities: are time-coordinates, still a single row could have multiple granularities
Aggregates: integrate Facts over Granularities together, thus reducing the cardinality
Technical Fields: are metadata or non-business attribute that support operations
6.2 Denormalization makes it easy to lose data or generate duplicates (but, it doesn't have to and we'll see...)
7.2 Should show the relations b/w entities, but require extra work (indices, normalization, sharding...) to optimize
12. Normalization reduces redundancies; thus making data less vulnerable to inconsistencies
1NF: - each column should hold a single value (no grouped values)
- no duplicate rows
2NF: - 1NF satisfied
- each non-key attribute must depend on the entire PK (replace a CompositeK w/ a SurrogateK)
10.2 If the upstream datasets are denormalized, re-normalizing could be a good starting point
11. Entity-Relationship Diagrams define an ontology/taxonomy of your data
11.1 This are usually DiGraph where directionality is defined in the PK -> FK constraint
11b. Time-series have a composite key with measures
11b.1 These kinds of tables usually describe the evolution of a single entity, and will be aggregated over a window to more coarse-grained granularities
13.1 Dimension Tables describe the actors and their attributes. EG. Customers, Products
13.2 Fact Tables describe events that involve the actors. EG. Puchases, Sales
15. PITFALL: M:M relationships are not well-handled in RDBMS
17. PITFALL: JOINs are the root cause of your problems.
15.1 L/R/I Joins can cause data loss when PK/FK is NULL
15.3 When two tables are joined, the resulting table loses the directionality of the relation b/w entities
15.2 FO Joins can mitigate the loss of data, yet can explode rows from one side or the other
15.1 introduce "association tables" aka "bridges" to reduce them to ( +---< B >---+ )
15.2 Bridges contains all the combinations of PKs of the two tables as FKs, which also form a composite key
16. Then we're done! Let's JOIN! (...that's were things go south)
12.1 This will guarantee you'll be safe from insertion and deletion anomalies
18. UNION BRIDGEs comes to rescue
19. 3 problems:
21. Bonus: multi-fact models & non-conformed granularities (Target & Sales)
22. Take-aways
23. Acknowledgements
20. Union Bridges solve the 3 problems because they are immune to duplicates
Keys

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primary_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Composite_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surrogate_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreinger_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Candidate_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alternate_key
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superkey
Dimensions
Facts/Metrics
Granularities
- temporal
columns
- more then
one temporaly
Aggregates
Technical
fields
- Future
fields
Indexes
Example of slide
- Fan Trap: Sales many-to-one Sales: duplicates row -> measure aggregation overcount
- Chasm Trap: two Dimension tables many-to-one to a third one -> quadratic explosion when denormalizing
- Loops: n tables can have at most n-1 relations. Additional ones form loops (hard to join)
